---
title: "Harmonic Analysis 03-29-19"
output: 
  html_document: 
    toc: false
    toc_float: true
    theme: cosmo
    css: style.css
---

\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb{C}}
\newcommand{\Z}{\mathbb{Z}}
\newcommand{\N}{\mathbb{N}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\<}{\left<}
\newcommand{\>}{\right>}
\newcommand{\ra}{\rightarrow}
\newcommand{\sinc}{\mathrm{sinc}}
\newcommand{\supp}{\mathrm{supp}}

# From MRA to wavelets:

Need a family $\{\psi_{j,k}\equiv2^{j/2}\psi(2^jx-k)\}_{j,k\in\Z}$ that is an ONB of $L^2(\R)$. This comes from an MRA: 

$$\dots\subset V_{-1}\subset V_0\subset V_1\subset\dots$$

with a scaling function $\varphi$ s.t. $\{\varphi(x-k)\}_{k\in\Z}$ is an ONB of $V_0$. This means that $\{\varphi_{j,k}\}_k$ is an ONB of $V_j$. Furthermore $V_{j+1}=V_j\oplus W_j$; for each $\varphi$ there is a corresponding $\psi$ s.t. $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_k$ is an ONB of $W_j$. Considering all possible $j$; $L^2(\R)=\overline{\bigoplus_j W_j}$ and $\{\psi_{j,k}\}_{j,k}$ is an ONB of $L^2(\R)$.

__Question:__ what is the relationship between $\varphi$ and $\psi$?

__Comment:__ we may think Fourier analysis is outdated given all this modern wavelet stuff. however, the Fourier transform is essential for constructing wavelets.

By the requirements of $\varphi$, we have

$$\int_\R\varphi(x-k)\varphi(x-l)dx=\delta_{kl}.$$

This is equivalent to $\sum_{k\in\Z}|\hat\varphi(\gamma+k)|^2=1$ (for any $\gamma\in\R$) since

__Lemma:__ Let $f\in L^2(\R)$. The family $\{f(x-k)\}_k$ is orthonormal iff

$$\sum_{k\in\Z}|\hat f(\gamma+n)|^2=1, \ \gamma\in\R$$

__Proof:__ Set $\tau_k$ the translation by $k$ operator so that $\tau_k f=f(x-k)$. By change of variables (and translation invariance of Lebesgue integration)

$$\left<\tau_kf,\tau_mf\right>=\left<\tau_{k-m}f,f\right>$$

The orthonormality of $\{\tau_kf\}$ is equivalent to 

$$\left<\tau_kf,f\right>=\delta_0(k)$$

$$=\left<\mathscr F(\tau_kf),\mathscr F(f)\right>=\int_\R\mathscr{F}(\tau_kf)\overline{\mathscr{F}(f)}d\gamma$$

$$=\int_\R e^{-2\pi ik\gamma}\hat f\overline{\hat f}d\gamma=\int_\R e^{-2\pi i k\gamma}|\hat f|^2d\gamma$$

$$=\sum_{n\in\Z}\int_n^{n+1}e^{-2\pi i k\gamma}|\hat f|^2d\gamma=\star$$

Let $\nu=\gamma-n$ for each interval $(n,n+1)$ in the partition of $\R$:

$$\star=\sum_n\int_0^1 e^{-2\pi i k(\nu+n)}|\hat f(\nu+n)|^2d\nu$$

$$=\sum_n\int_0^1 e^{-2\pi i k\nu}|\hat f(\nu+n)|^2d\nu$$

$$=\int_0^1e^{-2\pi i k\nu}\sum_n|\hat f(\nu+n)|^2d\nu=\star$$

The function $F(\nu)\equiv\sum_n|\hat f(\nu+n)|^2$ is 1-periodic and $\star$ calculates the $k$th Fourier coeff of $F(\nu)$ and is equal to $\delta_0(k)$.

Let $h\equiv1$ on $[0,1)$:

$$\hat h(k)=\int_0^1 e^{-2\pi ikt}dt=\left.\frac{e^{-2\pi ikt}}{-2\pi ik}\right|_0^1$$

$$=\frac{1-e^{-2\pi ik}}{2\pi ik}=\delta_0(k)$$

Thus $F=h$ (ae) by the uniqueness of Fourier transform. $\square$

#### Two important properties the scaling function must hold

  i. $\sum_{n\in\Z}|\hat\varphi(\gamma+n)|^2=1$
  
  ii. there is $\{h_k\}$ s.t. $\sum_{k\in\Z}|h_k|^2<\infty$ and
  
  $$\varphi(x)=\sqrt 2\sum_kh_k\varphi(2x-k)=\sum_k h_k\varphi_{1,j}$$

This is because $\varphi\in V_0\subset V_1$ and $\{\varphi_{1,k}\}$ is an ONB of $V_1$. Note $h_k=\left<\varphi,\varphi_{1,k}\right>$.

Take the Fourier transform of $\varphi$

$$\hat\varphi(\gamma)=\sum_k h_k\hat\varphi_{1,k}$$

$$\hat\varphi_{1,k}=\sqrt{2}e^{-2\pi ik\gamma}\frac{1}{2}\hat\varphi(\gamma/2)$$

$$=\frac{e^{-2\pi ik\gamma}}{\sqrt 2}\hat \varphi(\gamma/2)$$

and thus

$$\hat\varphi(\gamma)=\hat \varphi(\gamma/2)H(\gamma)$$

where $H(\gamma)=\frac{1}{\sqrt 2}\sum_kh_ke^{-2\pi ik\gamma}$ is a 1-periodic function, a trigonometric function.
